rawText:
One of the advantages of having kids is that when you have to give advice, you can ask yourself "what would I tell my own kids?" My kids are little, but I can imagine what I'd tell them about startups if they were in college, and that's what I'm going to tell you.

Startups are very counterintuitive. I'm not sure why. Maybe it's just because knowledge about them hasn't permeated our culture yet. But whatever the reason, starting a startup is a task where you can't always trust your instincts.

It's like skiing in that way. When you first try skiing and you want to slow down, your instinct is to lean back. But if you lean back on skis you fly down the hill out of control. So part of learning to ski is learning to suppress that impulse. Eventually you get new habits, but at first it takes a conscious effort. At first there's a list of things you're trying to remember as you start down the hill.

Startups are as unnatural as skiing, so there's a similar list for startups. Here I'm going to give you the first part of it — the things to remember if you want to prepare yourself to start a startup.

The first item on it is the fact I already mentioned: that startups are so weird that if you trust your instincts, you'll make a lot of mistakes. If you know nothing more than this, you may at least pause before making them.

When I was running Y Combinator I used to joke that our function was to tell founders things they would ignore. It's really true. Batch after batch, the YC partners warn founders about mistakes they're about to make, and the founders ignore them, and then come back a year later and say "I wish we'd listened."

Why do the founders ignore the partners' advice? Well, that's the thing about counterintuitive ideas: they contradict your intuitions. They seem wrong. So of course your first impulse is to disregard them. And in fact my joking description is not merely the curse of Y Combinator but part of its raison d'etre. If founders' instincts already gave them the right answers, they wouldn't need us. You only need other people to give you advice that surprises you. That's why there are a lot of ski instructors and not many running instructors.

You can, however, trust your instincts about people. And in fact one of the most common mistakes young founders make is not to do that enough. They get involved with people who seem impressive, but about whom they feel some misgivings personally. Later when things blow up they say "I knew there was something off about him, but I ignored it because he seemed so impressive."

If you're thinking about getting involved with someone — as a cofounder, an employee, an investor, or an acquirer — and you have misgivings about them, trust your gut. If someone seems slippery, or bogus, or a jerk, don't ignore it.

This is one case where it pays to be self-indulgent. Work with people you genuinely like, and you've known long enough to be sure.

sentences.original: One of the advantages of having kids is that when you have to give advice, you can ask yourself "what would I tell my own kids?"
sentences.translation: 아이를 갖는 것의 장점 중 하나는, 조언을 해야 할 때 "내 아이에게는 뭐라고 말해줄까?"라고 스스로에게 물을 수 있다는 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: One of the advantages of having kids
grammarElements.label: 주어 (One of + 복수명사)

grammarElements.text: that when you have to give advice
grammarElements.label: that절 안의 종속절 (시간 — when)

grammarElements.text: "what would I tell my own kids?"
grammarElements.label: 간접의문문 (목적어)

vocabulary.example: One advantage of remote work is flexibility.
She gave me good advice about my career.

expressions.usage: 여러 장점 중 하나를 소개할 때 사용하는 도입 표현

expressions.phrase: what would I tell my own kids?
expressions.usage: 가정법을 활용한 자기 성찰 표현. 진심 어린 조언의 기준을 제시

patterns.pattern: One of the [noun(plural)] of [gerund] is that ...
patterns.explanation: 어떤 행위의 특정 속성을 소개하는 패턴. 복수명사 중 하나를 꺼내는 구조.
examples: One of the benefits of exercising is that you sleep better.
One of the challenges of learning a language is that it takes time.

drillQuestions.question: One _____ the advantages of having kids is that you can give honest advice.
You can ask yourself what _____ I tell my own kids.

sentences.notes: Paul Graham의 에세이 특유의 오프닝: 개인적 경험에서 시작하여 보편적 주제로 넘어간다. "what would I tell my own kids?"는 가정법 과거로, 아직 아이가 어리지만 가정적으로 상상하는 것.

koreanAlignment.en: One of the advantages of having kids
koreanAlignment.ko: 아이를 갖는 것의 장점 중 하나는

koreanAlignment.en: is that when you have to give advice
"what would I tell my own kids?"

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 개인적 프레이밍(personal framing) — 부모의 관점을 빌려 조언의 진정성을 확보하며 시작

sentences.original: My kids are little, but I can imagine what I'd tell them about startups if they were in college, and that's what I'm going to tell you.
sentences.translation: 제 아이들은 어리지만, 그들이 대학생이라면 스타트업에 대해 뭐라고 말해줄지 상상할 수 있고, 그것이 제가 여러분에게 하려는 이야기입니다.

grammarElements.text: what I'd tell them about startups
grammarElements.label: 간접의문문 (목적어)

grammarElements.text: and that's what I'm going to tell you
grammarElements.label: 등위절 (결론)

vocabulary.example: Can you imagine living in another country?
She founded a tech startup in Silicon Valley.

expressions.phrase: that's what I'm going to tell you
expressions.usage: 강연이나 발표에서 앞으로의 내용을 예고할 때
가정법 과거 — 현재 사실과 반대되는 상황을 가정

patterns.pattern: I can imagine what I'd [verb] if [subject] [were/past tense] ...
patterns.explanation: 가정법 과거를 사용하여 현재 사실과 다른 상황을 상상하는 패턴.
examples: I can imagine what I'd say if I were in your shoes.
I can imagine what I'd do if I had more time.

drillQuestions.question: I can imagine what I'd tell them if they _____ in college.
That's _____ I'm going to tell you.

sentences.notes: "if they were in college"는 가정법 과거(subjunctive). 실제로는 아이들이 어리므로 비현실적 가정. I'd = I would. "that's what I'm going to tell you"로 에세이의 목적을 명확하게 선언한다.

koreanAlignment.en: what I'd tell them about startups
that's what I'm going to tell you

sentences.original: Startups are very counterintuitive. I'm not sure why.
sentences.translation: 스타트업은 매우 반직관적입니다. 왜 그런지 확실하지 않습니다.

vocabulary.example: It may seem counterintuitive, but exercising gives you more energy.

expressions.usage: 원인을 완전히 모를 때 겸손하게 인정하는 표현

patterns.pattern: [Subject] is/are very [adjective]. I'm not sure why.
patterns.explanation: 관찰 결과를 제시하고, 원인에 대한 불확실성을 솔직히 인정하는 패턴. 지적 정직함을 보여줌.
examples: This approach is very effective. I'm not sure why.
People are very resistant to change. I'm not sure why.

drillQuestions.question: Startups are very _____. They go against what you expect.
I'm not _____ why startups are counterintuitive.

sentences.notes: counterintuitive는 counter-(반대) + intuitive(직관적인)의 합성어. 이 에세이의 핵심 키워드로, 이후 반복적으로 등장한다. "I'm not sure why"는 Paul Graham 특유의 솔직한 화법.
pronunciationNotes: counterintuitive: /ˌkaʊntərɪnˈtuːɪtɪv/ — "카운터인투이티브", 강세는 세 번째 음절 tu
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 핵심어 선언(thesis statement) — 에세이의 중심 주제를 단순명료하게 선언

sentences.original: Maybe it's just because knowledge about them hasn't permeated our culture yet.
sentences.translation: 아마 그저 스타트업에 대한 지식이 아직 우리 문화에 스며들지 않았기 때문일 것입니다.

vocabulary.example: The smell of coffee permeated the entire house.
Knowledge of coding is increasingly important.

expressions.usage: 원인을 추측하되 확정하지 않을 때. 겸손한 가설 제시
현재완료 + yet으로 아직 완료되지 않은 과정을 표현

patterns.pattern: Maybe it's just because [subject] hasn't [past participle] ... yet
patterns.explanation: 아직 일어나지 않은 변화를 원인으로 추측하는 패턴. 현재완료 부정 + yet 구조.
examples: Maybe it's just because the technology hasn't matured yet.
Maybe it's just because the idea hasn't spread widely yet.

drillQuestions.question: Knowledge about startups hasn't _____ our culture yet.
Maybe it's _____ because we don't know enough.

sentences.notes: permeate는 라틴어 permeare(통과하다)에서 유래한 고급 어휘. 물이 스며들듯 지식이 문화에 천천히 퍼지는 이미지를 담고 있다. yet은 현재완료 부정문에서 "아직"의 의미.

sentences.original: But whatever the reason, starting a startup is a task where you can't always trust your instincts.
sentences.translation: 하지만 이유가 무엇이든, 스타트업을 시작하는 것은 항상 직관을 믿을 수 없는 과업입니다.

grammarElements.text: where you can't always trust your instincts
grammarElements.label: 관계부사절 (where — task를 수식)

vocabulary.example: Trust your instincts when making difficult decisions.
Learning a new language is a challenging task.

expressions.usage: 원인 분석을 접어두고 결론으로 넘어갈 때
직관이 틀릴 수 있음을 경고할 때. always의 위치에 주목 — "항상 믿을 수 없다"가 아니라 "늘 믿을 수 있는 건 아니다"

patterns.pattern: But whatever the [noun], [gerund phrase] is a [noun] where you can't ...
patterns.explanation: 원인을 제쳐두고 핵심 결론을 제시하는 패턴. whatever로 논점을 전환.
examples: Whatever the cause, managing people is a skill where you can't rely on textbooks alone.
Whatever the explanation, writing well is a craft where you can't skip practice.

drillQuestions.question: _____ the reason, starting a startup requires caution.
You can't always trust your _____ when starting a startup.

sentences.notes: "can't always"와 "always can't"는 의미가 다르다. "can't always trust" = "항상 믿을 수는 없다" (부분 부정). "always can't trust" = "늘 믿을 수 없다" (전면 부정). 이 문장은 부분 부정으로, 직관이 가끔은 맞지만 항상은 아니라는 뜻.

koreanAlignment.en: where you can't always trust your instincts

pronunciationNotes: instincts: /ˈɪnstɪŋkts/ — "인스팅크츠", nk 뒤 ts 발음에 유의

sentences.original: It's like skiing in that way.
sentences.translation: 그런 점에서 스키와 비슷합니다.

vocabulary.example: Skiing is a popular winter sport.

expressions.usage: 앞서 언급한 특성에 한정하여 비교할 때

patterns.pattern: It's like [noun/gerund] in that way.
patterns.explanation: 특정 측면에서의 유사성을 제시하는 비유 패턴. 복잡한 개념을 친숙한 것에 비유.
examples: Learning a language is like swimming in that way.
Investing is like gardening in that way.

drillQuestions.question: It's _____ skiing in that way.

sentences.notes: 짧지만 중요한 전환 문장. Paul Graham이 자주 사용하는 비유법(analogy)의 시작점. 스타트업이라는 추상적 개념을 스키라는 구체적 신체 활동에 비유한다.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 비유(analogy) — 추상적인 스타트업 개념을 구체적인 스키 경험에 비유하여 이해를 돕는다

sentences.original: When you first try skiing and you want to slow down, your instinct is to lean back.
sentences.translation: 처음 스키를 타면서 속도를 줄이고 싶을 때, 본능적으로 뒤로 기대게 됩니다.

grammarElements.text: and you want to slow down
grammarElements.label: 등위 종속절 (and)

vocabulary.example: He leaned back in his chair and relaxed.
You need to slow down on this curve.

expressions.usage: 자연스러운 반응이나 충동을 설명할 때

patterns.pattern: When you [verb] and you want to [verb], your instinct is to [verb].
patterns.explanation: 상황과 자연스러운 반응을 연결하는 패턴. 직관적 반응을 묘사.
examples: When you face criticism and you want to respond, your instinct is to defend yourself.
When you start a business and you want to grow, your instinct is to hire more people.

drillQuestions.question: When you want to slow down, your instinct is to lean _____.
Your _____ is to lean back when skiing.

sentences.notes: lean back은 스키에서 실제로 위험한 동작. 체중이 뒤로 가면 스키 컨트롤을 잃게 된다. 이것은 스타트업에서 직관을 따르면 실패한다는 비유의 구체적 예시.

koreanAlignment.en: and you want to slow down
your instinct is to lean back

sentences.original: But if you lean back on skis you fly down the hill out of control.
sentences.translation: 하지만 스키에서 뒤로 기대면 통제 불능 상태로 언덕을 내려가게 됩니다.

grammarElements.text: if you lean back on skis
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (if)

vocabulary.example: The car flew down the highway.
The situation was getting out of control.

expressions.usage: 상황이나 움직임을 제어할 수 없을 때

patterns.pattern: But if you [do the intuitive thing], you [negative consequence].
patterns.explanation: 직관적 행동이 역효과를 가져온다는 반전을 보여주는 패턴.
examples: But if you study harder without rest, you burn out faster.
But if you lower prices too much, you destroy your brand.

drillQuestions.question: If you lean back on skis, you fly down the hill _____ of control.

sentences.notes: "fly down"은 비유적 표현. 실제로 날지는 않지만 그만큼 빠르게, 통제할 수 없이 내려가는 것을 의미. 스키 비유의 핵심: 직관(뒤로 기대기)이 재앙(통제 불능)으로 이어진다.

koreanAlignment.en: But if you lean back on skis

sentences.original: So part of learning to ski is learning to suppress that impulse.
sentences.translation: 그러므로 스키를 배우는 것의 일부는 그 충동을 억제하는 법을 배우는 것입니다.

vocabulary.example: She tried to suppress her laughter.
He had a sudden impulse to quit his job.

expressions.phrase: part of learning to X is learning to Y
expressions.meaning: X를 배우는 일부는 Y를 배우는 것이다
expressions.usage: 기술 습득에 필수적인 하위 과정을 설명할 때

patterns.pattern: Part of [gerund A] is [gerund B].
patterns.explanation: 한 행위의 본질적 구성요소를 설명하는 패턴. 동명사가 주어와 보어 양쪽에 사용.
examples: Part of growing up is learning to accept failure.
Part of being a leader is knowing when to listen.

drillQuestions.question: Part of learning to ski is learning to _____ that impulse.
_____ of learning to ski is learning to suppress impulses.

sentences.notes: suppress는 sub-(아래로) + press(누르다)에서 유래. 충동을 "아래로 눌러" 억제한다는 이미지. 이 문장은 스키 비유의 교훈: 성공하려면 자연스러운 충동을 의식적으로 억제해야 한다.

sentences.original: Eventually you get new habits, but at first it takes a conscious effort.
sentences.translation: 결국에는 새로운 습관이 생기지만, 처음에는 의식적인 노력이 필요합니다.

vocabulary.example: She made a conscious decision to change her career.
He eventually got used to the new schedule.

expressions.usage: 자연스럽지 않은 행동을 하기 위해 의도적으로 노력해야 할 때
나중에 변화하는 초기 상태를 묘사할 때. eventually와 대조적으로 사용

patterns.pattern: Eventually you [verb], but at first it takes [noun].
patterns.explanation: 시간에 따른 변화를 대비하는 패턴. 초기 어려움과 최종 적응을 대조.
examples: Eventually you feel comfortable, but at first it takes courage.
Eventually you understand, but at first it takes patience.

drillQuestions.question: _____ you get new habits, but at first it takes effort.
At first it takes a _____ effort to override your instincts.

sentences.notes: eventually(결국) vs at first(처음에)의 시간적 대조. "it takes"는 비인칭 주어 구문으로 "~이 필요하다"라는 의미. conscious는 "의식이 있는"과 "의식적인" 두 가지 뜻이 있는데, 여기서는 후자.

sentences.original: Startups are as unnatural as skiing, so there's a similar list for startups.
sentences.translation: 스타트업은 스키만큼 부자연스러운 것이라, 스타트업에도 비슷한 목록이 있습니다.

vocabulary.example: It felt unnatural to type with my left hand.

expressions.usage: 두 대상의 동등한 정도를 비교할 때

patterns.pattern: [A] is as [adjective] as [B], so ...
patterns.explanation: 두 대상의 유사성을 동등비교로 확립한 뒤, 그로부터 결론을 도출하는 패턴.
examples: Management is as challenging as engineering, so there's a similar learning curve.
Writing is as demanding as coding, so there are similar principles to follow.

drillQuestions.question: Startups are _____ unnatural as skiing.
There's a _____ list for startups.

sentences.notes: as ... as 동등비교 구조. 스키 비유를 마무리하며 스타트업으로 논점을 돌려놓는 전환 문장. "unnatural"은 un-(부정) + natural(자연스러운) — 직관에 반한다는 핵심 메시지를 강화.

koreanAlignment.en: Startups are as unnatural as skiing
so there's a similar list for startups

pronunciationNotes: unnatural: /ʌnˈnætʃrəl/ — "언내추럴", nn이 연속이지만 한 번만 발음

sentences.original: Here I'm going to give you the first part of it — the things to remember if you want to prepare yourself to start a startup.
sentences.translation: 여기서 저는 그 목록의 첫 번째 부분을 알려드리겠습니다 — 스타트업을 시작할 준비를 하고 싶다면 기억해야 할 것들입니다.

grammarElements.text: if you want to prepare yourself to start a startup
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (if)

vocabulary.example: You should prepare yourself for unexpected challenges.

expressions.usage: 앞으로 전달할 내용을 예고할 때
to부정사가 things를 수식하는 형용사적 용법

patterns.pattern: Here I'm going to give you [X] — [explanation of X]
patterns.explanation: dash(—)를 사용하여 앞의 내용을 부연 설명하는 패턴. 강연/에세이의 로드맵 제시.
examples: Here I'm going to share the key insight — the one thing that changed everything.
Here I'm going to explain the process — the steps you need to follow.

drillQuestions.question: I'm going to give you the first _____ of it.
The things to remember _____ you want to start a startup.

sentences.notes: dash(—)는 앞의 표현을 풀어서 설명하는 역할. "the first part of it"이 무엇인지 dash 뒤에서 구체적으로 밝힌다. "prepare yourself to start a startup"에서 to부정사가 연쇄적으로 이어지는 구조.

koreanAlignment.en: Here I'm going to give you
if you want to prepare yourself to start a startup

sentences.original: The first item on it is the fact I already mentioned: that startups are so weird that if you trust your instincts, you'll make a lot of mistakes.
sentences.translation: 첫 번째 항목은 제가 이미 언급한 사실입니다: 스타트업은 너무나 이상해서, 직관을 믿으면 많은 실수를 하게 됩니다.

grammarElements.text: that if you trust your instincts, you'll make a lot of mistakes
grammarElements.label: so ... that 결과절

vocabulary.example: The whole situation felt weird.
He mentioned the problem during the meeting.

expressions.usage: 정도가 심하여 특정 결과가 발생함을 표현하는 구문
관계대명사 that/which가 생략된 구조. 이전 논점을 상기시킬 때

patterns.pattern: [Subject] is/are so [adjective] that if you [verb], you'll [consequence].
patterns.explanation: so...that 구문 안에 if 조건절을 넣어, 극단적 성질이 초래하는 조건부 결과를 설명.
examples: The market is so volatile that if you hesitate, you'll miss the opportunity.
The rules are so complex that if you don't study them, you'll get confused.

drillQuestions.question: Startups are _____ weird that you'll make mistakes if you trust your instincts.
If you trust your instincts, you'll make a lot of _____.

sentences.notes: 이 문장에서 that이 두 번 사용: 첫 번째는 fact의 동격절, 두 번째는 so...that 결과절. "the fact I already mentioned"에서 관계대명사 that이 생략되어 있다 (the fact [that] I already mentioned).

koreanAlignment.en: is the fact I already mentioned
that if you trust your instincts
you'll make a lot of mistakes

sentences.original: If you know nothing more than this, you may at least pause before making them.
sentences.translation: 이것 이상으로 아는 것이 없더라도, 최소한 실수하기 전에 잠시 멈출 수 있을 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: If you know nothing more than this
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (if)

vocabulary.example: He paused for a moment to think.
At least try before giving up.

expressions.usage: 최소한의 지식만으로도 도움이 된다는 것을 강조
성급한 행동을 자제하도록 조언할 때

patterns.pattern: If you know nothing more than [X], you may at least [verb] before [gerund].
patterns.explanation: 최소한의 지식이라도 행동 개선에 도움이 된다는 겸손한 조언 패턴.
examples: If you know nothing more than the basics, you may at least avoid the worst errors.
If you understand nothing more than this principle, you may at least hesitate before acting rashly.

drillQuestions.question: If you know nothing more _____ this, you may at least pause.
You may at least pause _____ making mistakes.

sentences.notes: "nothing more than this"에서 this는 "스타트업은 반직관적이다"라는 사실. "them"은 앞 문장의 mistakes를 지칭. may는 약한 가능성을 나타내며, at least로 기대치를 낮추면서도 실용적 가치를 강조.

koreanAlignment.en: If you know nothing more than this

koreanAlignment.ko: 최소한 잠시 멈출 수 있을 것입니다

sentences.original: When I was running Y Combinator I used to joke that our function was to tell founders things they would ignore.
sentences.translation: Y Combinator를 운영할 때 저는 우리의 역할은 창업자들이 무시할 것들을 말해주는 것이라고 농담하곤 했습니다.

grammarElements.text: When I was running Y Combinator
grammarElements.label: 종속절 (시간 — when)

grammarElements.text: that our function was to tell founders things
grammarElements.label: that절 (joke의 내용)

vocabulary.example: The function of the committee is to review proposals.
The founder of the company retired last year.
Don't ignore the warning signs.

expressions.usage: used to + 동사로 과거의 반복된 행동을 표현
관계대명사 생략 + would로 예상되는 미래 행동을 표현

patterns.pattern: I used to [verb] that our [role] was to [verb] ...
patterns.explanation: 과거 경험에서 반복적으로 했던 말이나 생각을 회상하는 패턴.
examples: I used to say that our job was to prepare students for the unexpected.
I used to think that my role was to make everyone happy.

drillQuestions.question: I _____ to joke that our function was to tell founders things.
Our function was to tell founders things they would _____.

sentences.notes: "used to"는 과거의 습관적 행동을 나타낸다 (would와 유사하지만 상태에도 사용 가능). "things they would ignore"에서 관계대명사 that이 생략. function은 여기서 "수학적 함수"가 아닌 "역할/기능"의 의미.

koreanAlignment.en: When I was running Y Combinator
our function was to tell founders

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 자기비하적 유머(self-deprecating humor) — 농담 형식을 빌려 깊은 통찰을 전달하는 Paul Graham 특유의 화법

sentences.original: Batch after batch, the YC partners warn founders about mistakes they're about to make, and the founders ignore them, and then come back a year later and say "I wish we'd listened."
sentences.translation: 기수마다, YC 파트너들이 창업자들에게 곧 저지를 실수에 대해 경고하면, 창업자들은 무시하고, 1년 뒤에 돌아와서 "들을걸 그랬다"라고 말합니다.

grammarElements.text: about mistakes they're about to make
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (관계대명사 목적격 생략)

grammarElements.text: and then come back a year later
grammarElements.label: 등위절 (and then)

vocabulary.example: The next batch of cookies is ready.
Doctors warned about the health risks.
I was about to leave when the phone rang.

expressions.usage: "X after X" 패턴으로 끊임없는 반복을 강조
I wish + 과거완료 — 과거에 하지 않은 일에 대한 후회

patterns.pattern: [X] after [X], [subject] [verb], and [subject] [verb], and then [verb] ... and say ...
patterns.explanation: 반복되는 패턴을 시간순으로 나열하여 좌절감을 표현. 등위접속사 and의 연쇄.
examples: Year after year, teachers explain the rules, students break them, and then come back asking for help.

drillQuestions.question: _____ after batch, the YC partners warn founders.
"I wish we'd _____," the founders say.

sentences.notes: "I wish we'd listened" = "I wish we had listened" — 가정법 과거완료로 과거에 대한 후회를 표현. "batch"는 YC 특유의 용어로, 한 기수(cohort)의 스타트업 그룹을 의미. 네 개의 등위절(warn, ignore, come back, say)이 사건의 반복적 흐름을 보여준다.

koreanAlignment.en: about mistakes they're about to make
and then come back a year later

sentences.original: Why do the founders ignore the partners' advice? Well, that's the thing about counterintuitive ideas: they contradict your intuitions.
sentences.translation: 왜 창업자들은 파트너의 조언을 무시할까요? 반직관적 아이디어는 원래 그렇습니다: 직관에 모순되니까요.

grammarElements.text: that's the thing about counterintuitive ideas
grammarElements.label: 주절 (답변)

vocabulary.example: The evidence contradicts his theory.
She relied on her intuition to make the decision.

expressions.usage: 어떤 것의 본질적 특성을 설명할 때. 구어적이면서도 핵심을 찌르는 표현

patterns.pattern: Why do [subject] [verb]? Well, that's the thing about [X]: they [verb].
patterns.explanation: 수사의문문을 던진 뒤 직접 답변하는 자문자답 패턴. 에세이에서 논증을 전개하는 효과적 방법.
examples: Why do people resist change? Well, that's the thing about habits: they feel comfortable.
Why do students procrastinate? Well, that's the thing about hard work: it's not immediately rewarding.

drillQuestions.question: That's the _____ about counterintuitive ideas: they contradict your intuitions.
Counterintuitive ideas _____ your intuitions.

sentences.notes: "that's the thing about X"는 Paul Graham이 자주 쓰는 구어적 표현으로, 핵심 특성을 간결하게 짚어낸다. colon(:)은 앞의 주장을 뒤에서 구체적으로 설명하는 역할.

koreanAlignment.en: Why do the founders ignore the partners' advice?
that's the thing about counterintuitive ideas

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 자문자답(rhetorical question + answer) — 독자의 의문을 예측하여 스스로 묻고 답하는 구조

sentences.original: If founders' instincts already gave them the right answers, they wouldn't need us.
sentences.translation: 창업자들의 직관이 이미 올바른 답을 줬다면, 그들은 우리가 필요하지 않았을 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: If founders' instincts already gave them the right answers
grammarElements.label: 가정법 과거 조건절

vocabulary.example: There are no easy right answers to this problem.

expressions.usage: 가정법으로 현실과 반대되는 상황을 표현하여 존재 가치를 역설

patterns.pattern: If [subject's noun] already [past tense], they wouldn't [verb].
patterns.explanation: 가정법 과거. 현재 사실의 반대를 가정하여 논리적 귀결을 보여주는 패턴.
examples: If students' textbooks already had all the answers, they wouldn't need teachers.
If customers already knew what they wanted, companies wouldn't need market research.

drillQuestions.question: If founders' instincts already gave them answers, they _____ need us.
Founders' instincts don't always give them the _____ answers.

sentences.notes: 가정법 과거(second conditional): "If + 과거형, would + 동사원형". 현재 사실의 반대를 가정. 실제로는 직관이 올바른 답을 주지 않기 때문에 YC가 필요하다는 논리.

koreanAlignment.en: If founders' instincts already gave them the right answers
koreanAlignment.ko: 창업자들의 직관이 이미 올바른 답을 줬다면

sentences.original: You only need other people to give you advice that surprises you. That's why there are a lot of ski instructors and not many running instructors.
sentences.translation: 놀라운 조언을 해줄 다른 사람이 필요한 것입니다. 그래서 스키 강사는 많고 달리기 강사는 많지 않은 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: to give you advice that surprises you
grammarElements.label: 목적격 보어 (to부정사 + 관계대명사절)

grammarElements.text: there are a lot of ski instructors
grammarElements.label: 존재 구문

vocabulary.example: The ski instructor taught us how to turn.
The results surprised everyone.

expressions.usage: 예상치 못한, 반직관적인 조언을 의미. 이 에세이의 핵심 주장을 함축
원인에서 결과로 논리를 연결할 때

patterns.pattern: You only need [X] to [verb]. That's why there are [A] and not [B].
patterns.explanation: 필요 조건을 제시한 뒤, 현실의 예시로 뒷받침하는 논증 패턴.
examples: You only need coaches for skills that are counterintuitive. That's why there are many golf coaches and not many walking coaches.

drillQuestions.question: You only need other people to give you advice that _____ you.
That's _____ there are a lot of ski instructors.

sentences.notes: 스키 비유의 결론부. 달리기는 직관적이라 강사가 거의 필요 없고, 스키는 반직관적이라 강사가 많다는 통찰. 이를 스타트업과 YC의 관계에 대입: 스타트업은 반직관적이므로 조언자(YC)가 필요하다.

koreanAlignment.en: to give you advice that surprises you
That's why there are a lot of ski instructors

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 비유 확장(extended analogy) — 스키 비유를 강사 비유로 확장하여 논증을 완성

sentences.original: You can, however, trust your instincts about people.
sentences.translation: 하지만, 사람에 대한 직관은 믿을 수 있습니다.

vocabulary.example: The plan seemed good. However, it failed.

expressions.usage: 특정 영역에서의 직관적 판단을 신뢰할 때

patterns.pattern: You can, however, [verb] your [noun] about [topic].
patterns.explanation: however를 삽입하여 앞 문장과의 대조를 부드럽게 표현하는 패턴. 동사 사이에 끼워넣는 구조.
examples: You can, however, rely on your judgment about character.
You should, however, trust your gut feeling about risks.

drillQuestions.question: You can, _____, trust your instincts about people.

sentences.notes: "however"가 동사구 중간에 삽입된 구조. "can"과 "trust" 사이에 위치하여 앞선 논의(직관을 믿지 마라)와의 대조를 만든다. 이것이 에세이의 핵심 전환: 비즈니스 직관은 믿지 말되, 사람에 대한 직관은 믿어라.

koreanAlignment.ko: 사람에 대한 직관은 믿을 수 있습니다

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 전환(pivoting) — 주요 논지의 예외를 제시하여 논의에 깊이를 더함

sentences.original: If you're thinking about getting involved with someone — as a cofounder, an employee, an investor, or an acquirer — and you have misgivings about them, trust your gut.
sentences.translation: 누군가와 관계를 맺으려 한다면 — 공동 창업자든, 직원이든, 투자자든, 인수자든 — 그리고 그들에 대한 불안한 느낌이 있다면, 직감을 믿으세요.

grammarElements.text: If you're thinking about getting involved with someone
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (if + 진행형)

grammarElements.text: as a cofounder, an employee, an investor, or an acquirer
grammarElements.label: 삽입구 (dash 사이 — 나열)

grammarElements.text: and you have misgivings about them
grammarElements.label: 등위 조건절 (and)

vocabulary.example: She had misgivings about the deal.
The acquirer offered a premium for the company.
My gut tells me this is a bad idea.
Don't get involved with shady business deals.

expressions.usage: 논리보다 본능적 판단을 따르라는 조언. 특히 사람을 판단할 때
확실한 근거 없이도 느끼는 불안한 예감을 표현

patterns.pattern: If you're thinking about [gerund] — [list of examples] — and you have [noun], [imperative].
patterns.explanation: dash를 사용한 삽입절과 함께 조건부 명령을 전달하는 고급 패턴.
examples: If you're considering a career move — in tech, finance, or consulting — and you feel uncertain, take your time.

drillQuestions.question: If you have _____ about someone, trust your gut.
If you're thinking about getting _____ with someone, be careful.

sentences.notes: dash(—) 사이의 나열(cofounder, employee, investor, acquirer)은 스타트업 생태계에서 만나는 주요 관계자들. misgivings는 mis-(잘못된) + giving(감정)에서 유래한 불안감을 뜻하는 고급 어휘. "trust your gut"은 "trust your instincts"의 더 구어적이고 강한 표현.

koreanAlignment.en: If you're thinking about getting involved with someone
as a cofounder, an employee, an investor, or an acquirer
and you have misgivings about them

sentences.original: If someone seems slippery, or bogus, or a jerk, don't ignore it.
sentences.translation: 누군가가 미끄러운(미심쩍은) 사람이거나, 가짜이거나, 재수 없는 사람 같으면, 무시하지 마세요.

grammarElements.text: slippery, or bogus, or a jerk
grammarElements.label: 보어 (3개 병렬)

vocabulary.example: He seemed slippery — always avoiding direct answers.
The certificate turned out to be bogus.
He acted like a complete jerk at the party.

expressions.usage: 불편한 직감을 행동으로 옮기라는 직설적 조언
seem으로 주관적 인상을 표현. 확정적 판단이 아닌 느낌 전달

patterns.pattern: If someone seems [A], or [B], or [C], don't [verb] it.
patterns.explanation: 부정적 인상을 나열한 뒤, 그것을 무시하지 말라는 명령. or의 반복으로 어떤 경우든 적용됨을 강조.
examples: If a deal seems too good, or unclear, or rushed, don't accept it.
If a plan seems risky, or vague, or unrealistic, don't commit to it.

drillQuestions.question: If someone seems slippery, or bogus, or a jerk, don't _____ it.
If someone seems _____, trust your gut.

sentences.notes: slippery, bogus, jerk — 세 단어 모두 구어적이고 직설적. Paul Graham의 글쓰기 특징인 "격식 없는 직접적 표현". slippery는 물리적(미끄러운)→비유적(교활한) 의미 전이의 예.

sentences.original: This is one case where it pays to be self-indulgent.
sentences.translation: 이것은 자기 자신에게 관대해지는 것이 이득인 경우입니다.

grammarElements.text: where it pays to be self-indulgent
grammarElements.label: 관계부사절 (where — case를 수식)

vocabulary.example: She admitted that buying the luxury car was self-indulgent.
It pays to be honest in the long run.

expressions.usage: 장기적으로 보상을 받는 행동을 추천할 때
예외적 상황을 강조할 때

patterns.pattern: This is one case where it pays to [be/verb] ...
patterns.explanation: 일반적 규칙의 예외를 소개하는 패턴. 특정 상황에서는 다른 접근이 필요함을 강조.
examples: This is one case where it pays to be patient.
This is one case where it pays to spend more money.

drillQuestions.question: This is one case where it _____ to be self-indulgent.
It pays to be _____-indulgent when choosing partners.

sentences.notes: self-indulgent는 보통 부정적 의미(방종적)이지만, 여기서 Paul Graham은 의도적으로 긍정적 맥락에서 사용. "함께 일할 사람 선택에서만큼은 자기 기분을 우선시해도 된다"는 뜻.

koreanAlignment.en: where it pays to be self-indulgent

sentences.original: Work with people you genuinely like, and you've known long enough to be sure.
sentences.translation: 진심으로 좋아하는 사람, 그리고 확신할 수 있을 만큼 충분히 오래 알아온 사람과 함께 일하세요.

grammarElements.text: and you've known long enough to be sure
grammarElements.label: 등위 관계대명사절 (and — people 수식)

vocabulary.example: She was genuinely surprised by the news.

expressions.meaning: 확신할 수 있을 만큼 충분히 오래
expressions.usage: enough + to부정사 구조. 충분한 기간을 통한 확신을 표현
관계 선택의 핵심 원칙을 간결하게 표현

patterns.pattern: [Imperative] with people you [verb A], and you've [verb B] long enough to [verb C].
patterns.explanation: 두 가지 조건(좋아함 + 충분한 시간)을 갖춘 사람과 함께하라는 명령 패턴.
examples: Partner with people you truly respect, and you've tested under pressure.
Hire people you naturally connect with, and you've seen perform consistently.

drillQuestions.question: Work with people you _____ like.
You've known them long _____ to be sure.

sentences.notes: 에세이 전반부의 마무리 문장. 관계대명사(whom/that)가 두 번 생략: "people [whom] you genuinely like" + "people [whom] you've known long enough". genuinely는 일상 대화보다 격식 있는 표현으로, "그냥 좋아하는" 것이 아닌 진심을 강조.

koreanAlignment.en: and you've known long enough to be sure
koreanAlignment.ko: 확신할 수 있을 만큼 충분히 오래 알아온

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 경구(aphorism) — 간결하고 기억하기 쉬운 실용적 조언으로 섹션을 마무리
